questions.question: Explain the difference between Aggregation and Composition with a real-world example in a university management system.
keyPoints: Aggregation: 'has-a' relationship, parts can exist independently
Composition: Stronger 'contains' relationship, parts cannot exist without whole
Example: Department-Aggregation (can move to another faculty), Course-Composition (cannot exist without department)
UML notation: Empty diamond for aggregation, filled diamond for composition

questions.question: What are CRC cards? Describe their structure and how they are used in the requirements analysis phase.
keyPoints: CRC = Class-Responsibility-Collaborator
Three sections: Class Name, Responsibilities, Collaborators
Used for object-oriented analysis and class discovery
Facilitates team discussion and early identification of classes
Iterative process during requirements gathering

questions.question: Define multiplicity in class diagrams and explain the following notations: 0..1, 1, 0..*, 1..*.
keyPoints: Multiplicity defines how many instances relate between classes
0..1 = Optional, at most one (e.g., each Employee has 0 or 1 parking slot)
1 = Exactly one (e.g., each Account belongs to exactly one Customer)
0..* = Zero to many, optional many (e.g., Customer has 0 to many Orders)
1..* = One to many, at least one (e.g., Order must have at least one Item)

questions.question: Describe the three sections of a UML class box and explain the visibility indicators (+, -, #).
keyPoints: Top: Class name (bold, centered)
Middle: Attributes (properties/data members)
Bottom: Operations/Methods (behaviors)
+ (plus): Public - accessible everywhere
- (minus): Private - accessible only within the class
# (hash): Protected - accessible in class and subclasses

questions.question: Explain the concept of Generalization (Inheritance) in class modeling with an example. How does it differ from Association?
keyPoints: Generalization: 'is-a' relationship (Student is a Person)
Subclass inherits attributes and operations from superclass
Promotes code reuse and establishes hierarchy
Association: 'uses' or 'knows' relationship without inheritance
Generalization is stronger coupling than association

questions.question: What is a Dependency relationship in UML? When should it be used? Give an example from an e-commerce system.
keyPoints: Dependency: One class uses another but doesn't 'hold' it permanently
Shown as dashed arrow in UML
Used when: class uses another as parameter, local variable, or return type
Example: Order class uses PaymentProcessor to process payments
Changes to PaymentProcessor might affect Order, but not vice versa

questions.question: Explain the process of identifying classes from requirements. What guidelines should be followed?
Points: Look for nouns in requirements (potential classes)
Identify attributes from adjectives describing nouns
Identify operations from verbs indicating actions
Apply filters: eliminate irrelevant objects, keep system boundary in mind
Group related attributes and operations into classes

questions.question: You are designing a library management system. Using class-based modeling principles, identify the main classes and draw their relationships. Consider: Members can borrow multiple books, each book has multiple copies, and late returns incur fines.
keyPoints: Identify classes: Member, Book, BookCopy, Loan, Fine
Relationships: Member (1) ─ (0..*) Loan (aggregation, member has loans)
Book (1) ─ (1..*) BookCopy (composition, copies belong to book)
Loan connects Member and BookCopy (association class)
Fine associated with Loan (composition - fine belongs to loan)
Define attributes: Member(name, id, membershipDate), Book(title, ISBN, author)
Define multiplicities correctly for each relationship

questions.question: For the N-Queens problem (placing N queens on N×N chessboard with no attacks), design the class model. Identify classes, their responsibilities, collaborations, and draw a class diagram showing relationships.
keyPoints: Classes: ChessBoard, Queen, Position
ChessBoard responsibilities: Initialize board, place queens, check if solution valid
Queen responsibilities: Know position, check attacks with other queens
Position: Store row and column coordinates
Self-association: Queen checks with other Queens
Use CRC cards to design responsibilities
Model as collaboration diagram showing Queen-Queres interaction
Operations: canAttack(Position), placeAt(Position), remove()

questions.question: Explain the difference between Association, Aggregation, Composition, and Generalization with UML notations. When would you choose one over the other? Provide examples for each.
keyPoints: Association: Basic 'uses' relationship, can be bidirectional (line)
Aggregation: 'has-a', parts can exist independently (empty diamond)
Composition: Strong 'contains', parts die with whole (filled diamond)
Generalization: 'is-a' inheritance relationship (triangle arrow)
Choose based on lifecycle dependency: composition > aggregation > association
Examples: Car-Engine (composition), Department-Professor (aggregation), Animal-Dog (generalization), Student-Course (association)
